1.introduction: Greetings and introductions are the first things you learn in French — how to say hello, goodbye, and introduce yourself politely. From the very first conversation French separates formal address (vous) from informal (tu), so getting these phrases right sets the tone for everything that follows.
learningObjectives: Use bonjour, bonsoir, salut and au revoir correctly
Introduce yourself with je m'appelle and ask someone's name
Choose between formal (vous) and informal (tu) greetings
Respond to comment ça va? and everyday courtesies

2.introduction: French numbers from 1 to 100 follow clear patterns until the seventies, where counting switches to a base-of-twenty style — soixante-dix is literally 'sixty-ten' and quatre-vingts is 'four-twenties'. Numbers are essential for prices, ages, dates and telling the time.
learningObjectives: Count and spell the numbers 1 to 100
Handle the tricky 70s, 80s and 90s (soixante-dix, quatre-vingts, quatre-vingt-dix)
Use numbers for prices, ages and quantities
Recognise numbers in both spoken and written French

3.introduction: Days, months and seasons let you talk about your schedule, birthdays and the weather. In French these words are not capitalised, and you pair seasons with specific prepositions such as en été (in summer) and au printemps (in spring).
learningObjectives: Name the seven days and twelve months in French
Talk about the four seasons with the correct prepositions
Give dates in the French order (day before month)
Distinguish lundi ('on Monday') from le lundi ('on Mondays')

4.introduction: The definite articles le, la, l' and les all mean 'the', but the form you choose depends on the noun's gender and number. French uses definite articles far more than English, including with general ideas and things you like or dislike.
learningObjectives: Match le, la, l' and les to a noun's gender and number
Use l' before nouns beginning with a vowel or silent h
Apply definite articles with general and abstract nouns
Tell singular and plural article forms apart

5.introduction: Indefinite articles (un, une, des) mean 'a', 'an' or 'some', while partitive articles (du, de la, de l') express an unspecified quantity — some bread, some water. Choosing correctly is one of the trickiest early skills, because English often uses no article at all.
learningObjectives: Use un, une and des for countable nouns
Use du, de la and de l' for uncountable quantities
Change the article to de in negative sentences
Tell indefinite and partitive articles apart in context

6.introduction: Every French noun is either masculine or feminine, and that gender shapes the articles, adjectives and pronouns around it. Some endings hint at gender (-tion is usually feminine, -age often masculine), but the safest habit is to learn each noun together with its article.
learningObjectives: Identify common masculine and feminine noun endings
Learn new nouns together with le or la
Predict gender from typical suffixes
Spot frequent exceptions to the usual patterns

7.introduction: Most French nouns form their plural by adding -s, but this -s is silent, so plurals are heard through the article (les, des) rather than the noun itself. A few endings follow special rules, such as bateau to bateaux and animal to animaux.
learningObjectives: Form regular plurals by adding a silent -s
Apply -x plurals for nouns ending in -eau and -eu
Change -al endings to -aux
Rely on plural articles to signal number in speech

8.introduction: Subject pronouns (je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles) replace the person or thing doing the action and determine the verb ending. French has two words for 'you' — informal tu and formal or plural vous — and uses on constantly to mean 'we' in everyday speech.
learningObjectives: Match each subject pronoun to its verb form
Choose between tu and vous appropriately
Use on as an informal 'we'
Distinguish ils and elles for mixed and single-gender groups

9.introduction: Regular -ER verbs are the largest and most predictable verb group in French, covering roughly 90% of all verbs. Once you know the endings -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent on the stem of a verb like parler, you can conjugate thousands of others.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -ER verbs in the present tense
Apply the endings -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent
Form the stem by dropping the -er ending
Use common -ER verbs such as parler, aimer and habiter

10.introduction: Regular -IR verbs such as finir add -iss- in the plural forms, giving the endings -is, -is, -it, -issons, -issez, -issent. This group includes many useful verbs about change and choice, like choisir and grandir.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -IR verbs in the present tense
Add the -iss- infix in the nous, vous and ils/elles forms
Use verbs such as finir, choisir and réfléchir
Contrast the -IR endings with the -ER group

11.introduction: Regular -RE verbs like vendre and attendre drop the -re and add -s, -s, nothing, -ons, -ez, -ent. The third-person singular takes no ending at all (il vend), which often surprises new learners.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -RE verbs in the present tense
Note the empty ending in the il/elle form
Use verbs such as vendre, attendre and répondre
Compare -RE endings with the -ER and -IR groups

12.introduction: Être (to be) and avoir (to have) are the two most important verbs in French: they are irregular, extremely common, and later build the perfect tense. Learning their present-tense forms by heart is one of the best early investments a learner can make.
learningObjectives: Conjugate être: suis, es, est, sommes, êtes, sont
Conjugate avoir: ai, as, a, avons, avez, ont
Use avoir for age (j'ai 12 ans) and possession
Use être for identity, nationality and description

13.introduction: Alongside être and avoir, a small set of irregular verbs — aller, faire, venir and prendre — appears constantly in everyday French. They don't follow the regular patterns, so they are worth memorising as high-frequency building blocks.
learningObjectives: Conjugate aller, faire, venir and prendre in the present
Use aller for movement and near-future plans
Use faire for activities, weather and sports
Recognise these verbs in common fixed expressions

14.introduction: French negation wraps around the verb: ne goes before it and a second word (pas, jamais, rien, plus) after it. Learning these pairs lets you say what you don't do, no longer do, or never do.
learningObjectives: Place ne...pas correctly around the verb
Use ne...jamais, ne...rien and ne...plus
Change the article to de after a negative
Answer questions in the negative

15.introduction: French adjectives agree with the noun they describe in gender and number, usually adding -e for feminine and -s for plural. Getting this agreement right is essential for natural-sounding French.
learningObjectives: Make adjectives agree in gender and number
Add -e for feminine and -s for plural
Handle adjectives already ending in -e
Recognise common irregular feminine forms

16.introduction: Most French adjectives come after the noun, but a small group of common ones (grand, petit, beau, bon) go before it. Knowing which is which keeps your descriptions correct.
learningObjectives: Place most adjectives after the noun
Put short common adjectives (beauty, age, goodness, size) before it
Use beau, nouveau and vieux before nouns
Spot adjectives that change meaning by position

17.introduction: This topic covers vocabulary for family members plus the possessive adjectives (mon, ma, mes) needed to talk about relatives. Possessives agree with the thing owned, not the owner.
learningObjectives: Name family members in French
Use possessives mon, ma, mes, ton, ta, tes
Match possessives to the gender of the noun owned
Describe relationships and marital status

18.introduction: Talking about food brings together vocabulary and the partitive articles (du, de la, des) for 'some'. You'll order, express preferences and describe meals.
learningObjectives: Name common foods, drinks and meals
Use partitive articles du, de la, de l', des
Express likes and dislikes with the definite article
Order food and drink politely

19.introduction: French tells time with il est + the hour, using et quart, et demie and moins le quart for the fractions. Both the 12-hour and 24-hour clocks are common.
learningObjectives: Ask and answer Quelle heure est-il?
Use et quart, et demie and moins le quart
Read the 24-hour clock used for schedules
Say at what time with à

20.introduction: Prepositions like à, de, en, dans, sur, sous and avec show location, direction and relationships. Some contract with articles (à + le = au, de + les = des).
learningObjectives: Use à, de, en, dans, sur, sous and avec
Form contractions au, aux, du, des
Show location and direction
Choose the right preposition with places

21.introduction: French offers three ways to ask a question: rising intonation, est-ce que, and inversion. They range from casual speech to more formal writing.
learningObjectives: Ask yes/no questions with intonation
Form questions with est-ce que
Use subject-verb inversion
Choose the right style for the situation

22.introduction: Weather expressions mostly use the verb faire (il fait beau, il fait froid), with a few using il y a and il pleut. This lets you describe conditions and seasons.
learningObjectives: Answer Quel temps fait-il?
Use faire for most weather expressions
Use il pleut, il neige and il y a du vent
Link weather to the seasons

23.introduction: This topic combines clothing vocabulary with colour adjectives, which agree with the noun. A few colours are invariable, a common exception.
learningObjectives: Name common items of clothing
Make colour adjectives agree in gender and number
Use the verb porter (to wear)
Note invariable colours like orange and marron

24.introduction: Body vocabulary is often used with the definite article rather than a possessive (j'ai mal à la tête). This topic also supports describing aches and pains.
learningObjectives: Name the main parts of the body
Use the definite article with body parts
Say avoir mal à to describe pain
Form contractions au and aux with à

25.introduction: Daily routines use reflexive verbs (se lever, se laver) where the action reflects back on the subject. The reflexive pronoun changes with each person.
learningObjectives: Conjugate reflexive verbs like se lever and se laver
Match reflexive pronouns me, te, se, nous, vous to the subject
Describe a daily routine in order
Place the reflexive pronoun before the verb

26.introduction: This review week pulls together the whole A1 syllabus — articles, genders, present-tense verbs, negation, adjectives and everyday vocabulary — a check of your beginner foundations before B1.
learningObjectives: Revise articles, noun gender and plurals
Consolidate present-tense verb conjugations
Apply negation and adjective agreement
Test your A1 vocabulary across topics

1.introduction: The passé composé is the main past tense for completed actions, formed with avoir plus a past participle. Most verbs use avoir, so this is the starting point.
learningObjectives: Form the passé composé with avoir + past participle
Build regular participles (-é, -i, -u)
Learn irregular participles (eu, fait, pris)
Use it for completed past actions

2.introduction: A specific set of verbs — mostly movement and change of state — form the passé composé with être rather than avoir. The mnemonic DR MRS VANDERTRAMP helps you remember them.
learningObjectives: Identify the être verbs (DR MRS VANDERTRAMP)
Form the passé composé with être + participle
Make the participle agree with the subject
Distinguish être and avoir verbs

3.introduction: With être the past participle agrees with the subject; with avoir it agrees with a preceding direct object. These agreement rules are a key B1 skill.
learningObjectives: Agree the participle with the subject for être verbs
Agree with a preceding direct object for avoir verbs
Add -e and -s endings correctly
Spot when no agreement is needed

4.introduction: The imparfait describes ongoing, habitual or background past actions. It is built from the nous present stem plus -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
learningObjectives: Form the imparfait from the nous present stem
Apply the endings -ais, -ait, -ions, -aient
Use it for habits, descriptions and background
Conjugate the irregular stem of être (ét-)

5.introduction: Choosing between the two past tenses is one of the biggest B1 challenges: passé composé for completed events, imparfait for ongoing or background. They often appear together in a story.
learningObjectives: Use passé composé for finished, specific actions
Use imparfait for descriptions and habits
Combine both tenses in a narrative
Interpret how the choice changes meaning

6.introduction: The plus-que-parfait ('had done') describes an action that happened before another past action. It uses the imparfait of avoir or être plus a past participle.
learningObjectives: Form the plus-que-parfait with imparfait of avoir/être
Express an action prior to another past action
Apply participle agreement rules
Use it in reported speech and si clauses

7.introduction: The future simple ('will do') adds endings to the infinitive: -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont. Many common verbs have irregular future stems (serai, aurai, irai).
learningObjectives: Form the future simple on the infinitive stem
Apply the endings -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont
Learn irregular stems (être, avoir, aller, faire)
Talk about future plans and predictions

8.introduction: The conditional ('would do') uses the future stem plus imparfait endings. It expresses politeness, hypotheticals and wishes (je voudrais).
learningObjectives: Form the conditional from the future stem + imparfait endings
Use it for polite requests (je voudrais, pourriez-vous)
Express hypothetical situations
Recognise irregular conditional stems

9.introduction: Direct object pronouns (me, te, le, la, nous, vous, les) replace the noun receiving the action and go before the verb, avoiding repetition of the object.
learningObjectives: Replace direct objects with le, la, les, etc.
Place the pronoun before the conjugated verb
Elide le/la to l' before a vowel
Agree the participle when the pronoun precedes

10.introduction: Indirect object pronouns (me, te, lui, nous, vous, leur) replace à + a person. Lui and leur are the third-person forms for both genders.
learningObjectives: Replace à + person with lui and leur
Distinguish indirect from direct object pronouns
Place the pronoun before the verb
Use with verbs like parler à and donner à

11.introduction: The pronoun y replaces à + place or thing, and en replaces de + noun or a quantity. Both go before the verb and are extremely common.
learningObjectives: Use y for à + place or thing
Use en for de + noun and quantities
Place y and en before the verb
Combine them with expressions of quantity

12.introduction: When several object pronouns appear together they follow a fixed order (me/te/se, then le/la/les, then lui/leur, then y, then en). Mastering this order makes speech flow.
learningObjectives: Order multiple pronouns correctly
Combine direct and indirect object pronouns
Add y and en in the right position
Place the cluster before the verb

13.introduction: Comparisons use plus...que, moins...que and aussi...que; superlatives use le/la plus and le/la moins. Bon and bien have irregular forms (meilleur, mieux).
learningObjectives: Form comparatives with plus/moins/aussi...que
Form superlatives with le plus and le moins
Use irregular meilleur and mieux
Agree superlative adjectives with the noun

14.introduction: Relative pronouns (qui, que, dont, où) join two clauses. Qui is the subject, que the object, dont replaces de, and où refers to place or time.
learningObjectives: Use qui as the subject of a relative clause
Use que as the direct object
Use dont for de + noun
Use où for place and time

15.introduction: The present subjunctive is formed from the ils present stem plus subjunctive endings. It appears after certain expressions rather than standing alone.
learningObjectives: Form the subjunctive from the ils present stem
Apply endings -e, -es, -e, -ions, -iez, -ent
Learn irregular subjunctives (être, avoir, aller, faire)
Recognise when the subjunctive is triggered

16.introduction: The subjunctive follows expressions of necessity, wish, emotion and doubt — il faut que, je veux que, bien que. It signals subjectivity rather than fact.
learningObjectives: Use the subjunctive after il faut que and je veux que
Use it after emotion and doubt
Use it after conjunctions like bien que and pour que
Contrast it with the indicative

17.introduction: The imperative gives commands and suggestions using the tu, nous and vous forms without the subject pronoun. -ER verbs drop the -s in the tu form.
learningObjectives: Form commands in the tu, nous and vous forms
Drop the subject pronoun
Remove the -s from tu forms of -ER verbs
Attach pronouns after affirmative commands (parle-moi)

18.introduction: The passive voice uses être plus a past participle that agrees with the subject, with the agent introduced by par. It shifts focus onto the receiver of the action.
learningObjectives: Form the passive with être + past participle
Agree the participle with the subject
Introduce the agent with par
Convert active sentences to passive

19.introduction: Reported (indirect) speech relays what someone said, requiring changes to pronouns, tenses and time words. Present becomes imparfait and future becomes conditional.
learningObjectives: Convert direct quotes to indirect speech
Shift tenses back (present to imparfait)
Adjust pronouns and time expressions
Introduce statements with que and questions with si

20.introduction: Conditional 'if' sentences follow fixed patterns: Type 1 (si + present, future) for real possibilities and Type 2 (si + imparfait, conditional) for hypotheticals.
learningObjectives: Form Type 1 si clauses (si + present, future)
Form Type 2 si clauses (si + imparfait, conditional)
Match the tense in each half
Express real vs hypothetical conditions

21.introduction: Many French adverbs are formed by adding -ment to the feminine adjective (heureuse becomes heureusement). Their placement usually follows the conjugated verb.
learningObjectives: Form adverbs with -ment from adjectives
Place adverbs after the conjugated verb
Use common irregular adverbs (bien, mal, vite)
Position adverbs with compound tenses

22.introduction: Logical connectors (cependant, pourtant, en revanche, d'ailleurs) link ideas and show contrast, addition or consequence, making writing more cohesive at B1 level.
learningObjectives: Use contrast connectors cependant and pourtant
Use en revanche and par contre
Add ideas with d'ailleurs and de plus
Structure arguments cohesively

23.introduction: This topic builds vocabulary for trips, transport and directions, alongside the prepositions used with countries and cities (en France, au Canada, à Paris).
learningObjectives: Use travel and transport vocabulary
Choose prepositions for countries and cities
Ask for and give directions
Handle common travel situations

24.introduction: Health vocabulary lets you describe symptoms and visit a doctor, often using avoir mal à and reflexive verbs like se sentir. This is practical B1 communication.
learningObjectives: Describe symptoms with avoir mal à
Use se sentir to say how you feel
Name common ailments and remedies
Communicate at a pharmacy or doctor's

25.introduction: Professional vocabulary covers jobs, workplaces and formal communication. Note that French drops the article when stating a profession (je suis professeur).
learningObjectives: Name jobs and workplaces
State a profession without an article
Use a formal register for work contexts
Talk about tasks and responsibilities

26.introduction: This review consolidates the B1 syllabus — the past tenses, pronouns, subjunctive and si clauses — before moving toward C1, checking that intermediate structures are secure.
learningObjectives: Revise passé composé, imparfait and future
Consolidate object pronouns and y/en
Apply the subjunctive and si clauses
Test B1 vocabulary across themes

1.introduction: Greetings and introductions are your first German conversations — saying hallo, guten Tag and tschüss, and introducing yourself with ich heiße. German separates the formal Sie from the informal du, a choice you make from the very first hello.
learningObjectives: Use hallo, guten Tag, guten Morgen and tschüss
Introduce yourself with ich heiße or ich bin
Choose between formal Sie and informal du
Ask wie heißt du? and wie geht es dir?

2.introduction: German numbers are regular but read the units before the tens from 21 onward — einundzwanzig is literally 'one-and-twenty'. Numbers are essential for prices, ages, phone numbers and time.
learningObjectives: Count and spell numbers 1 to 100
Read units before tens (einundzwanzig = 21)
Use numbers for prices, ages and quantities
Recognise spoken and written numbers

3.introduction: Days, months and seasons let you talk about your schedule and the weather. You use am with days (am Montag) and im with months and seasons (im Januar, im Sommer).
learningObjectives: Name the seven days and twelve months
Talk about the four seasons
Use am with days and im with months and seasons
Give dates in German

4.introduction: German has three genders, so 'the' is der (masculine), die (feminine) or das (neuter), and die for all plurals. The article you learn with a noun tells you its gender.
learningObjectives: Use der, die and das for the three genders
Use die for all plural nouns
Learn nouns together with their article
Recognise gender from the article

5.introduction: The indefinite article 'a/an' is ein for masculine and neuter nouns and eine for feminine. Its negative form kein means 'not a' or 'no'.
learningObjectives: Use ein for masculine and neuter nouns
Use eine for feminine nouns
Form the negative with kein and keine
Contrast definite and indefinite articles

6.introduction: Every German noun is masculine, feminine or neuter, and gender affects articles, pronouns and adjective endings. Some endings are reliable — words in -ung and -heit are feminine, -chen are neuter.
learningObjectives: Identify masculine, feminine and neuter nouns
Use endings like -ung and -heit (feminine) and -chen (neuter) as clues
Learn each noun with der, die or das
Remember that all German nouns are capitalised

7.introduction: German plurals take several forms — adding -e, -er, -n/-en, -s, or an umlaut — so plurals are learned noun by noun. In the plural, the article is always die.
learningObjectives: Recognise the main plural patterns (-e, -er, -n/-en, -s)
Add umlauts where required (Mann to Männer)
Use die for all plural nouns
Learn plurals together with each noun

8.introduction: Personal pronouns (ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie, Sie) show who is doing the action. German has three words for 'you': informal du and ihr, and formal Sie, always capitalised.
learningObjectives: Match each pronoun to its verb form
Choose du, ihr or Sie correctly
Distinguish sie (she/they) from Sie (formal you)
Use the right pronoun for singular and plural

9.introduction: Regular German verbs add the endings -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en to the stem (mach-, spiel-). This one pattern covers the majority of verbs.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense
Apply the endings -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en
Form the stem by dropping -en
Use verbs like machen, spielen and lernen

10.introduction: Some common verbs change their stem vowel in the du and er/sie/es forms — fahren becomes du fährst, sprechen becomes du sprichst. The endings stay regular.
learningObjectives: Recognise a-ä, e-i and e-ie stem changes
Apply changes only in the du and er/sie/es forms
Conjugate fahren, sprechen, lesen and sehen
Keep the regular endings

11.introduction: Sein (to be) and haben (to have) are the most important German verbs — irregular, very common, and later used to build the Perfekt tense. Learn their present forms by heart.
learningObjectives: Conjugate sein: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind
Conjugate haben: habe, hast, hat, haben, habt, haben
Use haben for possession and age
Use sein for identity and description

12.introduction: A handful of high-frequency verbs — gehen, kommen, wissen, werden — appear constantly and don't follow the regular pattern, so they are worth memorising early.
learningObjectives: Conjugate gehen, kommen, wissen and werden
Use werden for 'to become' and the future
Recognise these verbs in everyday phrases
Distinguish them from regular verbs

13.introduction: The nominative is the case of the subject — the person or thing doing the action. It uses the base article forms der, die, das, and is how every noun is listed in the dictionary.
learningObjectives: Identify the subject of a sentence
Use nominative articles der, die, das, die
Use the nominative after sein
Recognise nominative pronouns

14.introduction: The accusative marks the direct object — the thing receiving the action. Only the masculine article changes: der becomes den (Ich sehe den Mann).
learningObjectives: Identify the direct object
Change der to den in the accusative
Keep die, das and plural die unchanged
Use accusative pronouns (mich, dich, ihn)

15.introduction: This topic builds a core set of descriptive adjectives and their opposites (groß/klein, gut/schlecht). Used after sein, adjectives take no ending.
learningObjectives: Learn common adjectives and their opposites
Use adjectives after sein without endings
Describe people and things
Build vocabulary for descriptions

16.introduction: Colours are everyday adjectives used to describe objects. After a verb like sein they stay unchanged (das Auto ist rot); before a noun they take endings, introduced later.
learningObjectives: Name the common colours in German
Use colours after sein without endings
Describe objects by colour
Combine colours with nouns

17.introduction: Family vocabulary combines with possessive articles (mein, dein) that follow the ein/eine pattern. This lets you talk about your relatives.
learningObjectives: Name family members in German
Use possessives mein, meine, dein, deine
Match possessives to gender and case
Describe your family

18.introduction: Food vocabulary supports ordering and talking about meals, often with the verbs essen and trinken (essen is stem-changing). It is practical everyday German.
learningObjectives: Name common foods and drinks
Use the verbs essen and trinken
Order food and drink
Talk about meals and preferences

19.introduction: German tells the time with Wie spät ist es? and es ist..., using both the everyday half-based system (halb drei = 2:30) and the 24-hour clock for schedules.
learningObjectives: Ask and answer Wie spät ist es?
Use halb, Viertel and vor/nach
Read the 24-hour clock
Say at what time with um

20.introduction: A fixed set of prepositions — durch, für, gegen, ohne, um — always takes the accusative case, changing der to den after them.
learningObjectives: Learn the accusative prepositions (durch, für, gegen, ohne, um)
Change articles to the accusative after them
Use them in common phrases
Distinguish them from dative prepositions

21.introduction: Another fixed set — aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu — always takes the dative, changing der and das to dem and die to der.
learningObjectives: Learn the dative prepositions (aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu)
Change articles to the dative after them
Use common contractions (zum, zur, beim)
Contrast them with accusative prepositions

22.introduction: German question words (wer, was, wo, wann, warum, wie) start information questions, with the verb in second position. They are essential for everyday conversation.
learningObjectives: Use wer, was, wo, wann, warum and wie
Keep the verb in second position
Ask for people, things, places and reasons
Form open questions

23.introduction: Weather is described with es ist (es ist kalt) and es plus a verb (es regnet, es schneit). This lets you talk about conditions and the seasons.
learningObjectives: Answer Wie ist das Wetter?
Use es ist plus an adjective for conditions
Use es regnet and es schneit
Link weather to the seasons

24.introduction: Clothing vocabulary combines with shopping phrases and the verb tragen (to wear, stem-changing). You will ask for prices and sizes.
learningObjectives: Name common items of clothing
Use tragen (to wear) and kaufen (to buy)
Ask about price and size
Handle a simple shopping conversation

25.introduction: Modal verbs (können, müssen, wollen, dürfen, sollen, mögen) express ability, necessity and wishes. They send the main verb, as an infinitive, to the end of the sentence.
learningObjectives: Conjugate the six modal verbs
Send the second verb to the end as an infinitive
Express ability, necessity and permission
Use ich möchte for polite wishes

26.introduction: This review pulls together the A1 syllabus — articles, gender, present-tense verbs, the nominative and accusative cases, and everyday vocabulary — before moving to B1.
learningObjectives: Revise the three genders and their articles
Consolidate present-tense conjugation
Apply the nominative and accusative cases
Test A1 vocabulary across topics

1.introduction: The Perfekt is the main spoken past tense, formed with haben plus a past participle at the end of the sentence. Regular participles follow ge-...-t (gemacht), and many strong verbs use ge-...-en.
learningObjectives: Form the Perfekt with haben plus a past participle
Build regular participles (ge-...-t)
Learn strong participles (gegessen, gesehen)
Place the participle at the end

2.introduction: Verbs of movement or change of state form the Perfekt with sein instead of haben (ich bin gegangen). Learning which verbs take sein is a key B1 step.
learningObjectives: Identify verbs that take sein (movement, change of state)
Form the Perfekt with sein plus a participle
Use gegangen, gefahren and gekommen
Distinguish sein and haben verbs

3.introduction: In writing and with a few common verbs German prefers the Präteritum (simple past). Sein, haben and the modal verbs use this tense even in speech (war, hatte, konnte).
learningObjectives: Conjugate war (sein) and hatte (haben)
Form the Präteritum of modal verbs
Use these forms in everyday speech
Recognise the written past tense

4.introduction: The Präteritum is the standard past tense in narratives and writing. Regular verbs add -te (machte); strong verbs change their stem vowel (ging, kam, sah).
learningObjectives: Form the regular Präteritum with -te endings
Learn strong-verb stem changes (gehen to ging)
Use the tense for written narration
Contrast Präteritum with Perfekt

5.introduction: The Plusquamperfekt ('had done') describes an action before another past action, using the Präteritum of haben or sein plus a participle (ich hatte gegessen).
learningObjectives: Form the Plusquamperfekt with hatte or war plus a participle
Express an action prior to another past event
Combine it with nachdem clauses
Apply the correct auxiliary

6.introduction: The future tense uses werden plus an infinitive at the end (ich werde lernen). It expresses future plans and predictions, though the present often covers the near future.
learningObjectives: Form Futur I with werden plus an infinitive
Place the infinitive at the end
Express plans and predictions
Compare it with the present for the future

7.introduction: The dative marks the indirect object — the recipient — and follows certain verbs (helfen, danken) and prepositions. Articles become dem, der, dem and den, and plural nouns add -n.
learningObjectives: Identify the indirect object
Use dative articles dem, der, dem, den
Learn dative verbs like helfen and danken
Add -n to plural nouns in the dative

8.introduction: The genitive shows possession ('of the'), using des/der/des/der and adding -s or -es to masculine and neuter nouns (das Auto des Mannes). It is common in formal and written German.
learningObjectives: Use genitive articles des, der, des, der
Add -s or -es to masculine and neuter nouns
Express possession and 'of' relationships
Use genitive prepositions (wegen, während, trotz)

9.introduction: Nine prepositions (in, an, auf, über, unter, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen) take the accusative for movement toward a place and the dative for a fixed location — Wohin? versus Wo?.
learningObjectives: Learn the nine two-way prepositions
Use the accusative for movement (Wohin?)
Use the dative for location (Wo?)
Form contractions like ins, im, ans, am

10.introduction: Adjectives before a noun take endings that depend on the article type (strong, weak or mixed) and the case. Mastering these endings is a defining B1 skill.
learningObjectives: Choose weak endings after definite articles
Choose mixed endings after ein-words
Use strong endings with no article
Match endings to gender, case and number

11.introduction: Comparatives add -er (schneller) and superlatives use am ...-sten (am schnellsten). Common words are irregular (gut, besser, am besten).
learningObjectives: Form comparatives with -er
Form superlatives with am ...-sten
Use als for comparisons (schneller als)
Learn irregular forms (gut, viel, gern)

12.introduction: Reflexive verbs use a reflexive pronoun that refers back to the subject (ich wasche mich). Some take the accusative, some the dative, and many describe feelings or daily routine.
learningObjectives: Use accusative reflexive pronouns (mich, dich, sich)
Conjugate verbs like sich waschen and sich freuen
Recognise dative reflexives
Describe routines and feelings

13.introduction: Konjunktiv II expresses hypotheticals, wishes and politeness, most often with würde plus an infinitive, plus the common forms wäre and hätte.
learningObjectives: Form Konjunktiv II with würde plus an infinitive
Use wäre and hätte
Make polite requests (könnten Sie...)
Express wishes and hypotheticals

14.introduction: Unreal 'if' sentences pair a wenn-clause in Konjunktiv II with a main clause using würde or a subjunctive form (Wenn ich Zeit hätte, würde ich kommen).
learningObjectives: Build unreal conditionals with wenn plus Konjunktiv II
Use würde in the main clause
Express unreal present and past conditions
Order the clauses correctly

15.introduction: Relative clauses add information about a noun using relative pronouns (der, die, das) whose form depends on gender and case. The verb goes to the end of the clause.
learningObjectives: Choose relative pronouns by gender and case
Send the verb to the end of the clause
Use commas to set off the clause
Combine two sentences into one

16.introduction: The passive uses werden plus a past participle to focus on the action rather than the doer (das Haus wird gebaut). The agent, if named, follows von.
learningObjectives: Form the passive with werden plus a participle
Focus on the action, not the doer
Introduce the agent with von
Use the passive in different tenses

17.introduction: Infinitive clauses with zu (ich hoffe, dich zu sehen) let you link ideas without a new subject. The construction um...zu expresses purpose.
learningObjectives: Form infinitive clauses with zu
Use um...zu to express purpose
Place zu plus the infinitive at the end
Recognise verbs that trigger zu-clauses

18.introduction: Subordinate clauses introduced by conjunctions like weil, dass or wenn send the conjugated verb to the end of the clause — a core feature of German word order.
learningObjectives: Introduce clauses with weil, dass, wenn and ob
Send the conjugated verb to the end
Punctuate with a comma
Combine main and subordinate clauses

19.introduction: This topic focuses on the verb-final rule and how the whole sentence reorders when a subordinate clause comes first, requiring inversion in the main clause.
learningObjectives: Apply the verb-final rule in subordinate clauses
Invert the main clause when the sub-clause comes first
Place separable prefixes correctly
Order multiple verbs at the end

20.introduction: German distinguishes coordinating conjunctions (und, aber, oder, denn), which keep normal word order, from subordinating ones (weil, obwohl), which send the verb to the end.
learningObjectives: Use coordinating conjunctions (und, aber, oder, denn)
Keep normal word order after them
Use subordinating conjunctions (weil, obwohl, dass)
Send the verb to the end after subordinators

21.introduction: Adverbs describe how, when and where, and German orders them by the rule time-manner-place. Their position affects emphasis.
learningObjectives: Use adverbs of time, manner and place
Order them time-manner-place
Use common adverbs (immer, oft, gestern)
Position adverbs for emphasis

22.introduction: This topic builds vocabulary for trips and transport, alongside the prepositions mit (by, with the dative) and nach or zu for destinations.
learningObjectives: Use travel and transport vocabulary
Say mit dem Zug and mit dem Bus
Use nach for cities and countries and zu for places
Ask for and follow directions

23.introduction: Health vocabulary lets you describe symptoms and visit a doctor, often using the dative (mir ist schlecht) and body-part words.
learningObjectives: Name parts of the body
Describe symptoms (ich habe Kopfschmerzen)
Use dative expressions like mir tut ... weh
Communicate at the doctor's or pharmacy

24.introduction: Professional vocabulary covers jobs and workplaces. German states a profession without an article (ich bin Lehrer) and forms feminine titles with -in (Lehrerin).
learningObjectives: Name jobs and workplaces
State a profession without an article
Form feminine job titles with -in
Talk about tasks and careers

25.introduction: A group of masculine nouns (der Junge, der Student) add -n or -en in every case except the nominative singular. These 'weak nouns' are a classic B1 detail.
learningObjectives: Identify N-declension (weak) masculine nouns
Add -n or -en outside the nominative singular
Use them correctly in all four cases
Learn common examples (Junge, Student, Herr)

26.introduction: This review consolidates the B1 syllabus — the past tenses, all four cases, adjective endings, subordinate clauses and Konjunktiv II — before moving toward C1.
learningObjectives: Revise Perfekt, Präteritum and Plusquamperfekt
Consolidate the four cases and adjective endings
Apply subordinate-clause word order
Use Konjunktiv II and the passive

1.introduction: Greetings and introductions are your first Spanish conversations — hola, buenos días, adiós — and introducing yourself with me llamo. Spanish distinguishes the informal tú from the formal usted from the very first hello.
learningObjectives: Use hola, buenos días, buenas tardes and adiós
Introduce yourself with me llamo or soy
Choose between informal tú and formal usted
Ask ¿cómo te llamas? and ¿cómo estás?

2.introduction: Spanish numbers are regular after 30, but the teens and twenties have special one-word forms (dieciséis, veintiuno). Numbers are essential for prices, ages and time.
learningObjectives: Count and spell numbers 1 to 100
Use the special forms for 16-29 (dieciséis, veintidós)
Use numbers for prices, ages and quantities
Recognise numbers in speech and writing

3.introduction: Days, months and seasons let you talk about your schedule and the weather. In Spanish they are not capitalised, and you use el or los with days (el lunes) to mean 'on Monday'.
learningObjectives: Name the seven days and twelve months
Talk about the four seasons
Use el lunes and los lunes for 'on Monday(s)'
Give dates in Spanish

4.introduction: Spanish articles agree with the noun in gender and number: el, la, los, las mean 'the' and un, una, unos, unas mean 'a' or 'some'. Choosing the right one depends on gender.
learningObjectives: Use el, la, los, las for 'the'
Use un, una, unos, unas for 'a' or 'some'
Match articles to gender and number
Recognise gender from the article

5.introduction: Most Spanish nouns are masculine if they end in -o and feminine if they end in -a, though there are important exceptions (el día, la mano). Gender affects articles and adjectives.
learningObjectives: Use -o (masculine) and -a (feminine) as a guide
Learn exceptions (el día, la mano, el problema)
Match articles and adjectives to gender
Learn each noun with its article

6.introduction: Spanish forms plurals by adding -s after a vowel and -es after a consonant (libro to libros, ciudad to ciudades). The article also becomes plural.
learningObjectives: Add -s after a vowel and -es after a consonant
Change the article to los or las
Adjust spelling (luz to luces)
Make the whole phrase agree in number

7.introduction: Subject pronouns (yo, tú, él, ella, nosotros, vosotros, ellos) are often dropped in Spanish because the verb ending already shows the subject. Spain uses vosotros; Latin America uses ustedes.
learningObjectives: Match subject pronouns to verb endings
Understand why pronouns are often omitted
Choose tú, usted, vosotros or ustedes
Distinguish él/ella and ellos/ellas

8.introduction: Regular -AR verbs are the biggest verb group. Drop -ar and add -o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an to conjugate verbs like hablar, trabajar and estudiar.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -AR verbs in the present
Apply the endings -o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an
Form the stem by dropping -ar
Use hablar, trabajar and estudiar

9.introduction: Regular -ER verbs drop -er and add -o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en (comer, beber, leer). The endings differ from -AR only in the vowel.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -ER verbs in the present
Apply the endings -o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en
Use comer, beber and leer
Contrast -ER with -AR endings

10.introduction: Regular -IR verbs drop -ir and add -o, -es, -e, -imos, -ís, -en (vivir, escribir, abrir). They match -ER except in the nosotros and vosotros forms.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular -IR verbs in the present
Apply the endings -o, -es, -e, -imos, -ís, -en
Use vivir, escribir and abrir
Note where -IR differs from -ER

11.introduction: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be': ser for permanent traits, identity and time, and estar for location, feelings and temporary states. Choosing correctly is a defining early skill.
learningObjectives: Conjugate ser (soy, eres, es) and estar (estoy, estás, está)
Use ser for identity, origin and time
Use estar for location and feelings
Contrast permanent and temporary states

12.introduction: High-frequency verbs like tener, ir, hacer and poder are irregular and appear constantly, so they are worth memorising early. Tener and ir also form useful expressions.
learningObjectives: Conjugate tener, ir, hacer and poder
Use tener for age and possession (tengo 12 años)
Use ir a plus an infinitive for the near future
Recognise these verbs in common phrases

13.introduction: Spanish adjectives agree with the noun in gender and number, usually ending in -o/-a and adding -s for plural. Adjectives normally come after the noun.
learningObjectives: Make adjectives agree in gender and number
Place most adjectives after the noun
Handle adjectives ending in -e or a consonant
Form plural adjectives with -s or -es

14.introduction: Colours are common adjectives that agree with the noun (un coche rojo, una casa roja). This topic supports describing objects and people.
learningObjectives: Name the common colours in Spanish
Make colour adjectives agree with the noun
Describe objects and people
Combine colours with other adjectives

15.introduction: Family vocabulary combines with possessive adjectives (mi, tu, su, nuestro) that agree in number. Masculine plurals can include both genders (los padres = parents).
learningObjectives: Name family members in Spanish
Use possessives mi, tu, su, nuestro
Agree possessives in number
Use masculine plurals for mixed groups

16.introduction: Food vocabulary supports ordering and talking about meals, often with querer and the verb gustar. It is practical everyday Spanish.
learningObjectives: Name common foods and drinks
Order food with quiero and me gustaría
Talk about the meals of the day
Express food preferences

17.introduction: Spanish tells time with ¿Qué hora es? and es la una or son las..., using y and menos for minutes (son las tres y media). The 24-hour clock is used for schedules.
learningObjectives: Ask and answer ¿Qué hora es?
Use es la una and son las dos
Add minutes with y and menos (y media, menos cuarto)
Say at what time with a las

18.introduction: Prepositions like en, de, a, con, por and para show place, direction and relationships. De and a contract with el (de + el = del, a + el = al).
learningObjectives: Use en, de, a, con, por and para
Form the contractions del and al
Show location and direction
Choose the right preposition in context

19.introduction: Spanish question words (qué, quién, dónde, cuándo, cómo, cuánto) carry a written accent and open information questions, framed by inverted punctuation.
learningObjectives: Use qué, quién, dónde, cuándo, cómo and cuánto
Write the accent on question words
Use inverted question marks (¿...?)
Ask for people, things, places and reasons

20.introduction: Weather uses hacer (hace calor, hace frío), plus hay and está for some conditions. This lets you talk about the weather and seasons.
learningObjectives: Answer ¿Qué tiempo hace?
Use hace for most weather expressions
Use llueve, nieva and está nublado
Link weather to the seasons

21.introduction: Clothing vocabulary combines with shopping phrases and the verb llevar (to wear). You will ask for prices, sizes and colours.
learningObjectives: Name common items of clothing
Use llevar (to wear) and comprar (to buy)
Ask about price and size (¿cuánto cuesta?)
Handle a simple shopping conversation

22.introduction: Body vocabulary is often used with the definite article and reflexive verbs (me duele la cabeza) rather than a possessive. It supports describing aches and pains.
learningObjectives: Name the main parts of the body
Use the definite article with body parts
Say me duele and me duelen for pain
Describe symptoms simply

23.introduction: Daily routines use reflexive verbs (levantarse, ducharse) with a reflexive pronoun (me, te, se) before the verb. They describe actions done to oneself.
learningObjectives: Use reflexive pronouns me, te, se, nos, os
Conjugate levantarse and ducharse
Describe a daily routine in order
Place the reflexive pronoun before the verb

24.introduction: The verb gustar works backwards: what you like is the subject, and you are the indirect object (me gusta el café). Encantar and interesar follow the same pattern.
learningObjectives: Use me gusta (singular) and me gustan (plural)
Add indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, les)
Use a mí and a ti for emphasis and clarity
Apply the pattern to encantar and interesar

25.introduction: Hay means 'there is' or 'there are' and never changes form. Demonstratives (este, ese, aquel) point to things by distance and agree in gender and number.
learningObjectives: Use hay for 'there is' and 'there are'
Use este, ese and aquel by distance
Agree demonstratives in gender and number
Combine hay with quantities

26.introduction: This review pulls together the A1 syllabus — articles, gender, present-tense verbs, ser vs estar, gustar and everyday vocabulary — before moving to B1.
learningObjectives: Revise articles, gender and plurals
Consolidate present-tense conjugation
Apply ser vs estar and gustar
Test A1 vocabulary across topics

1.introduction: The preterite is the past tense for completed actions. Regular verbs add -é, -aste, -ó for -AR and -í, -iste, -ió for -ER/-IR. It reports finished events.
learningObjectives: Conjugate regular preterite -AR verbs
Conjugate regular -ER and -IR preterite verbs
Use it for completed past actions
Note the accents on -é and -ó

2.introduction: Many common verbs are irregular in the preterite, with special stems and endings (hice, tuve, estuve). Ser and ir share the same preterite forms (fui).
learningObjectives: Learn irregular stems (hacer to hic-, tener to tuv-)
Use the irregular endings -e, -iste, -o
Recognise that ser and ir share fui
Use estar, hacer and tener in the past

3.introduction: The imperfect describes ongoing, habitual or background past actions. Its endings are very regular (-aba for -AR; -ía for -ER/-IR), with only three irregular verbs.
learningObjectives: Form the imperfect with -aba and -ía endings
Use it for habits, descriptions and background
Learn the three irregulars (ser, ir, ver)
Contrast it with the preterite

4.introduction: Choosing between the two past tenses is a major B1 skill: preterite for completed events, imperfect for ongoing or background. They often appear together in a story.
learningObjectives: Use the preterite for finished, specific actions
Use the imperfect for descriptions and habits
Combine both in a narrative
Interpret how the choice changes meaning

5.introduction: The present perfect (he hablado) links the past to the present, formed with haber plus a past participle. Regular participles end in -ado for -AR and -ido for -ER/-IR.
learningObjectives: Form the present perfect with haber plus a participle
Build regular participles (-ado, -ido)
Learn irregular participles (hecho, dicho, visto)
Use it for recent or relevant past actions

6.introduction: The future ('will do') adds -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án to the infinitive. Some common verbs have irregular stems (tendré, haré, podré).
learningObjectives: Form the future on the infinitive stem
Apply the endings -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án
Learn irregular stems (tener, hacer, poder)
Express plans and predictions

7.introduction: The conditional ('would do') uses the same stems as the future plus imperfect endings (-ía). It expresses politeness, hypotheticals and wishes.
learningObjectives: Form the conditional with future stems plus -ía endings
Use it for polite requests (me gustaría, podría)
Express hypothetical situations
Share the irregular stems with the future

8.introduction: Direct object pronouns (me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las) replace the noun receiving the action and go before the conjugated verb. Lo and la agree in gender.
learningObjectives: Replace direct objects with lo, la, los, las
Place the pronoun before the conjugated verb
Attach it to infinitives and gerunds
Agree lo and la with gender

9.introduction: Indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, les) show to or for whom. Le and les often appear with a redundant a plus person for clarity (le doy el libro a Ana).
learningObjectives: Use le and les for the indirect object
Add a plus person for clarity
Place the pronoun before the verb
Distinguish direct and indirect pronouns

10.introduction: When both pronouns appear, the indirect comes before the direct, and le/les becomes se before lo/la/los/las (se lo doy). Getting the order right is a key B1 step.
learningObjectives: Put the indirect pronoun before the direct
Change le/les to se before lo and la
Attach both to infinitives and commands
Use se lo and se la correctly

11.introduction: Beyond daily routine, reflexive verbs express change of state, reciprocity and emotion (aburrirse, quejarse). The pronoun changes with the subject and can attach to infinitives.
learningObjectives: Use reflexive pronouns across all persons
Express change of state and emotion
Handle reciprocal actions (se escriben)
Attach the pronoun to infinitives and gerunds

12.introduction: Comparisons use más/menos...que and tan...como; superlatives use el or la más. Some words are irregular (bueno to mejor, malo to peor).
learningObjectives: Form comparatives with más/menos...que
Use tan...como for equality
Form superlatives with el más and el menos
Learn irregular mejor and peor

13.introduction: Por and para both translate as 'for', but por marks cause, exchange and duration, while para marks purpose, destination and deadlines. This contrast is a classic B1 challenge.
learningObjectives: Use para for purpose, destination and deadlines
Use por for cause, exchange and duration
Learn fixed expressions (por favor, para siempre)
Choose correctly in context

14.introduction: At B1, ser and estar are contrasted with adjectives that change meaning (es aburrido = boring; está aburrido = bored). The choice conveys real differences.
learningObjectives: Contrast ser and estar with adjectives
Learn meaning changes (aburrido, listo, rico)
Use estar for results and conditions
Use ser for essential qualities

15.introduction: The present subjunctive is formed from the yo present stem plus 'opposite' endings (-AR verbs take -e; -ER/-IR take -a). It appears in dependent clauses, not alone.
learningObjectives: Form the subjunctive from the yo present stem
Apply the opposite endings (-e for -AR; -a for -ER/-IR)
Learn irregular subjunctives (sea, vaya, haya)
Recognise when it is triggered

16.introduction: The subjunctive follows expressions of wish and emotion when the subject changes — quiero que vengas, espero que estés bien. It signals influence over another person.
learningObjectives: Use the subjunctive after quiero que and espero que
Change subjects between the clauses
Use it after emotion (me alegro de que)
Contrast it with the infinitive for the same subject

17.introduction: The subjunctive also follows doubt and denial — no creo que, dudo que, es posible que. When the speaker is certain, the indicative is used instead.
learningObjectives: Use the subjunctive after no creo que and dudo que
Use it after impersonal expressions (es posible que)
Switch to the indicative when certain
Recognise doubt as the trigger

18.introduction: Commands (the imperative) tell people what to do. Affirmative tú uses the él form (habla), while usted, ustedes and all negatives use subjunctive forms.
learningObjectives: Form affirmative tú commands (habla, come)
Use subjunctive forms for usted and ustedes
Form negative commands with no plus the subjunctive
Attach or place pronouns correctly

19.introduction: Relative pronouns (que, quien, donde, lo que) join clauses. Que is the most common; quien refers to people; lo que means 'what' or 'that which'.
learningObjectives: Use que to join clauses
Use quien for people after a preposition
Use donde for places
Use lo que for 'what' or 'that which'

20.introduction: Spanish often avoids the true passive, preferring the passive se (se venden casas) and the impersonal se (se habla español) to state actions without a subject.
learningObjectives: Form the passive se (se venden casas)
Use the impersonal se (se dice que...)
Contrast it with the true passive (ser + participle)
Choose the natural Spanish structure

21.introduction: Many Spanish adverbs are formed by adding -mente to the feminine adjective (rápida to rápidamente). Adverbial phrases (con cuidado) are common alternatives.
learningObjectives: Form adverbs with -mente from adjectives
Use common adverbs (bien, mal, siempre)
Replace adverbs with phrases (con cuidado)
Position adverbs naturally

22.introduction: Logical connectors (sin embargo, además, por lo tanto, aunque) link ideas and show contrast, addition and consequence, making writing more cohesive at B1.
learningObjectives: Use contrast connectors (sin embargo, aunque)
Add ideas with además and también
Show consequence with por lo tanto
Structure arguments cohesively

23.introduction: This topic builds vocabulary for trips and transport, alongside the prepositions used for movement (en autobús, a pie) and destinations.
learningObjectives: Use travel and transport vocabulary
Say en tren, en avión and a pie
Ask for and give directions
Handle common travel situations

24.introduction: Health vocabulary lets you describe symptoms and visit a doctor, often using doler like gustar (me duele) and reflexive verbs. It is practical B1 communication.
learningObjectives: Describe symptoms with me duele and tengo
Use doler like gustar
Name common ailments and remedies
Communicate at the pharmacy or doctor's

25.introduction: Professional vocabulary covers jobs and workplaces. Spanish states a profession without an article (soy profesor) and forms feminine titles (profesora).
learningObjectives: Name jobs and workplaces
State a profession without an article
Form feminine job titles
Talk about tasks and responsibilities

26.introduction: This review consolidates the B1 syllabus — the past tenses, object pronouns, por vs para, and the subjunctive — before moving toward C1.
learningObjectives: Revise preterite, imperfect and present perfect
Consolidate object pronouns and por vs para
Apply the subjunctive for wishes and doubt
Test B1 vocabulary across themes
